core_takeaway: The first position does not look payoff-advantaged. Agent_1 helped introduce the final Alpha template, but that template gave Quill to Agent_2 and the stronger non-Quill bundle to Agent_3, leaving Agent_1 with Apple+Stone. The last speaker beat the first because Agent_3 repeatedly vetoed zero-share offers and ultimately secured Jewel+Pencil, while Agent_1 accepted the lower 18-value bundle to reach consensus.
why_or_counterexample: This is a counterexample to first-position advantage. All agents had identical preferences, so Agent_1's lower payoff was not caused by taste differences. Agent_1 spoke first and even proposed the final allocation shape early, but the durable agreement centered on Agent_2 holding Quill and Agent_3 receiving a credible 33-value non-Quill stake. The bargaining power came from consensus pressure, Agent_3's veto against zero allocations, and Agent_2's pursuit of Quill, not from speaking first.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion began with every agent claiming Quill, but Agent_1 quickly shifted to Alpha, where Agent_2 gets Quill, Agent_1 gets Apple+Stone, and Agent_3 gets Jewel+Pencil. Later public rounds repeated that baseline and framed Stone/Pencil rebalancing as a fairness fallback, but the final public agreement stayed with the 18-vs-33 non-Quill split.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking shows Agent_1 and Agent_2 increasingly treated Alpha as the practical path, while Agent_3 initially preferred Quill but later accepted Alpha if Agent_3 got a tangible non-Quill stake. The hidden reasoning is about avoiding deadlock through defensible bundles, not protecting the first speaker.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals repeatedly tested Alpha. Some middle-round proposals overreached by giving Agent_2 Quill plus other non-Quill items and leaving Agent_3 empty; those failed. Round 5 converged when all three agents submitted the identical allocation: Agent_1 Apple+Stone, Agent_2 Quill, Agent_3 Jewel+Pencil.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private votes show self-utility and veto power driving outcomes. Agent_1 rejected its own early 18-value proposal when a 33-value alternative was available, Agent_2 rejected Quill-only offers when higher self-utility was possible, and Agent_3 rejected any zero allocation. Consensus arrived only once Agent_3 received Jewel+Pencil.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections repeatedly named non-Quill fairness and Agent_3's tangible stake as the gating issues. By the final round, the agents treated Alpha plus credible non-Quill bundles as the fastest path to unanimity, which advantaged Agent_3 over Agent_1 despite Agent_1 speaking first.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker created the final template, but not a first-payoff advantage
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first formal proposal already had the final allocation shape. That is evidence of agenda-setting, but it is weak as a first-position advantage because the template gives Agent_1 the low Apple+Stone bundle and gives Agent_3 the stronger Jewel+Pencil bundle.

evidence.quote: Quill is allocated to Agent_2 as the anchor (Option Alpha).

mechanisms.theme: Identical preferences make the lower first payoff a real allocation effect
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 and Agent_3 had the same item values, yet the final allocation gave Agent_3 Jewel+Pencil while Agent_1 got Apple+Stone. The last position's higher utility therefore reflects bargaining and allocation, not different valuations.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker's Quill claim did not stick
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 opened by naming Quill as the top priority, but public bargaining quickly centered on Agent_2 as the Quill holder. The first speaker did not convert first position into the top item.

evidence.quote: - Priority order: Quill (highest) > Pencil > Stone > Jewel > Apple.
Proposed anchor for Round 2: Alpha (Quill to Agent_2)

mechanisms.theme: Agent_3 veto power forced a meaningful last-position stake
mechanisms.explanation: The last speaker repeatedly rejected allocations that gave Agent_3 nothing. Reflections and later votes treated that veto as a central constraint, and final consensus only arrived when Agent_3 received Jewel+Pencil.

evidence.quote: I receive no items under this proposal (Agent_3 gets nothing), so my utility is 0 after the round discount.
Agent_3 keeps getting no items under the current anchors.

mechanisms.theme: Private votes followed utility comparisons rather than speaker order
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 rejected its own early final-shaped proposal because it only gave 18, while Agent_2 rejected fairer Quill-only proposals when a higher 82-value option existed. These votes show that position order was secondary to payoff and veto incentives.

evidence.quote: Proposal 1 yields Agent_1 only Apple+Stone (18 total)
I’m rejecting 1 in favor of the higher personal payoff in 3.
Reject: It gives me only Quill (49 pre-discount

mechanisms.theme: Fairness reflections centered non-Quill credibility, especially for Agent_3
mechanisms.explanation: The reflection sections repeatedly diagnosed the blocker as unfair or zero non-Quill shares, not the need to preserve Agent_1's opening demand. That diagnosis pushed the final deal toward Agent_3's credible bundle.

evidence.quote: distributing the non-Quill pool fairly is the real bottleneck.
The fastest path to consensus is to guarantee a credible non-Quill share for Agent_3
guarantee Agent_3 (and Agent_1) a credible non-Quill stake

mechanisms.theme: Final convergence required identical Alpha proposals and unanimous acceptance
mechanisms.explanation: Consensus was not produced by unilateral first-speaker leverage. It occurred only after every agent submitted the same allocation and every private vote accepted it.
